summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the public availability of ZFS, calling it the most important revolution in storage software in two decades. He traces the filesystem's origins to Jeff Bonwick's ambitious vision five years earlier and highlights ZFS's three pillars: dramatically simplified administration, unprecedented reliability through detection and correction of hardware errors, and excellent performance achieved through smarter software with more context. The post serves as a comprehensive tour of blog entries from the ZFS development team explaining various features and the grueling effort behind the release.
key_points: ZFS was designed from the ground up around unreliable hardware, detecting and correcting errors that other filesystems silently propagate
GUI admin tools should make something already simple even simpler, not smear lipstick over hideously over-complicated systems
Traditional storage reliability came from expensive hardware compensating for dumb software; ZFS inverts this with smart software enabling cheap hardware
ZFS delivers great performance because it has more context, a consequence of the end-to-end principle
Features like quotas, reservations, and ACLs were designed into ZFS from the start, not carpet-bombed over the source after the fact
money_quotes: I remember thinking 'he's either missed something basic that somehow invalidates these ideas – or this is the most important development in storage since RAID.'
ZFS is the most important revolution in storage software in two decades – and may be the most important idea since the filesystem itself.
GUI admin tools should make something that's already simple even simpler. They should not be used to smear lipstick over a hideously over-complicated system.
We've compensated for dumb software by having smart (and expensive) hardware. ZFS flips the economics on its head: smart software allows for stupid (and cheap) hardware – with ultra-high reliability.
How much of your data is being corrupted today? Yeah – scary, ain't it?
Like quotas and reservations, ACLs were a part of the design of ZFS – not something that was carpet-bombed over the source after the fact.
key_insight: Revolutionary systems software comes from starting with a blank sheet of paper, designing around real-world constraints like unreliable hardware, and inverting traditional economics by making software smarter rather than demanding expensive hardware.
